_comment: Demo worklist seeded from a live Claude Topic Analyst run (claude-opus-4-8) on docs-search-api @8d57340. exercise_plans are live ClaudeAnalyst-generated easy + medium plans for all three topics, captured verbatim so the Debug-to-Own MCQs load instantly and deterministically and match each topic-specific sandbox (hard is sandbox-only and needs no plan). The curated seed carries code-anchor evidence only; the Agent trace is sourced live from the repo's real ~/.claude transcripts via `make extract-claude` (no committed transcript fixture). Regenerate by re-running extraction / a live check; do not hand-edit.

topics.title: Tenant isolation depends on the pre-rerank visibility filter
topics.summary: The store returns candidates across every tenant by design, so the pipeline MUST scope them to the requesting tenant via visible_documents_for_tenant before any result escapes. Preserve the filter's behavior, its placement immediately after store.candidates() and before rerank, and its predicate on doc.tenant_id; do not weaken it to a pass-through or move it downstream.
topics.why_now: Highest priority: the store is deliberately tenant-blind (store.py docstring), so this single filter is the only thing enforcing tenant isolation. A silent refactor (e.g. to list(documents) or a renamed helper) compiles cleanly and may pass shallow tests while breaking the security boundary.
topics.impact_consequence: If the filter is removed, weakened, or reordered, one tenant's documents leak into another tenant's search results — a cross-tenant data disclosure.
topics.priority_rationale: Highest priority: the store is deliberately tenant-blind (store.py docstring), so this single filter is the only thing enforcing tenant isolation. A silent refactor (e.g. to list(documents) or a renamed helper) compiles cleanly and may pass shallow tests while breaking the security boundary.

revision.invariant: Every document in a SearchResponse satisfies doc.tenant_id == request.tenant_id; the tenant-blind candidate set is never returned unfiltered.

evidence.title: retrieval/store.py:28
evidence.relevance: candidates() is explicitly tenant-blind and matches on query tokens only, delegating tenant scoping to the pipeline.

evidence.title: retrieval/rerank.py:18
evidence.relevance: visible_documents_for_tenant is the sole enforcement point of tenant isolation; its predicate filters on doc.tenant_id.

evidence.title: retrieval/pipeline.py:55
evidence.relevance: search() calls visible_documents_for_tenant on the raw candidates before rerank, establishing the required ordering.

evidence.title: retrieval/models.py:16
evidence.body:
id: str
    tenant_id: str
    score: float
    text: str
evidence.relevance: Document carries tenant_id specifically so the pipeline can scope a shared-corpus result back to one caller.

topics.title: Confidence cutoff threshold is a single-source-of-truth policy
topics.summary: Keep MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE as the one named place defining the drop threshold, and keep apply_confidence_cutoff running before context packing so weak matches never reach context construction. Do not reintroduce the threshold as a bare literal elsewhere or relax the comparison.
topics.why_now: A threshold constant is intentionally centralized to avoid scattered literals; the value (0.30) and the cutoff's position before packing are deliberate policy, but the consequence is quality degradation rather than a correctness/security break.
topics.impact_consequence: Lowering, duplicating, or bypassing the threshold lets low-confidence matches into results and context, degrading answer quality and wasting the character budget on weak documents.
topics.priority_rationale: A threshold constant is intentionally centralized to avoid scattered literals; the value (0.30) and the cutoff's position before packing are deliberate policy, but the consequence is quality degradation rather than a correctness/security break.

revision.invariant: No document with score < MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE appears in the packed context or final results.

evidence.title: retrieval/rerank.py:25
evidence.relevance: rerank sorts by (-score, id) so ordering is fully deterministic and independent of candidate arrival order.

evidence.title: retrieval/pipeline.py:35
evidence.body:
def deduplicate(documents: list[Document]) -> list[Document]:
    """Collapse duplicate chunks, keeping the first occurrence of each id.

    The shared corpus can surface the same chunk more than once. Duplicates are
    resolved here, before the context window's size limit is applied, so the
    limit is spent on distinct documents. Because this runs after reranking, the
    first occurrence of an id is also its highest-scoring one.
    """
    seen: set[str] = set()
    unique: list[Document] = []
    for document in documents:
        if document.id in seen:
            continue
        seen.add(document.id)
        unique.append(document)
    return unique
evidence.relevance: deduplicate keeps the first occurrence per id and documents that this is correct only because it runs after reranking.

evidence.title: retrieval/pipeline.py:53
evidence.body:
def search(store: DocumentStore, query: str, tenant_id: str) -> SearchResponse:
    """Run the retrieval pipeline for ``query`` on behalf of ``tenant_id``."""
    candidates = store.candidates(query)
    scoped = visible_documents_for_tenant(candidates, tenant_id)
    ranked = rerank(scoped)
    deduped = deduplicate(ranked)
    confident = apply_confidence_cutoff(deduped)
    packed = pack_context(confident)
    hits = [
        SearchHit(id=document.id, score=document.score, excerpt=excerpt_for(document))
        for document in packed
    ]
    return SearchResponse(tenant_id=tenant_id, query=query, results=hits)
evidence.relevance: search() fixes the stage sequence scope -> rerank -> dedupe -> cutoff -> pack that the other stages depend on.

evidence.title: retrieval/rerank.py:12
evidence.body:
# Documents scoring below this confidence threshold are dropped before context
# construction. Named here so the policy lives in one place rather than as a
# bare literal scattered through the pipeline.
MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE = 0.30
evidence.relevance: MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE is the named single location for the confidence policy, intentionally not a scattered literal.

evidence.title: retrieval/rerank.py:34
evidence.relevance: apply_confidence_cutoff enforces the >= minimum_score filter that excludes weak matches.

topics.title: Context packing is bounded so no single document dominates the window
topics.summary: Preserve the two bounds: CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET caps the whole window and MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS caps each document's contribution. excerpt_for and pack_context must both clamp to MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS, and packing must remain a rank-ordered prefix that stops at the first document that would exceed the budget.
topics.why_now: These bounds protect window size and fairness across documents and are coupled (excerpt_for and pack_context both rely on the per-excerpt cap), but a violation degrades result composition rather than correctness or isolation.
topics.impact_consequence: Dropping the per-excerpt cap lets one large document overflow or monopolize the window; breaking the prefix/early-stop logic packs lower-ranked documents over higher-ranked ones, changing which results the caller sees.
topics.priority_rationale: These bounds protect window size and fairness across documents and are coupled (excerpt_for and pack_context both rely on the per-excerpt cap), but a violation degrades result composition rather than correctness or isolation.

revision.invariant: Total packed characters <= CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET, each excerpt <= MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS, and the packed set is a prefix of the reranked input.

evidence.title: retrieval/context.py:14
evidence.body:
# Total size of the packed context window, in characters.
CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET = 600
# No single excerpt may contribute more than this many characters, so one large
# document cannot dominate the window or overflow it on its own.
MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS = 200
evidence.relevance: CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET and MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS define the window and per-excerpt bounds, with the rationale that one document cannot dominate or overflow.

evidence.title: retrieval/context.py:21
evidence.relevance: excerpt_for clamps a document's representation to MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS, the same cap packing accounts for.

evidence.title: retrieval/context.py:26
evidence.body:
def pack_context(
    documents: list[Document],
    *,
    char_budget: int = CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET,
    max_excerpt: int = MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS,
) -> list[Document]:
    """Select the rank-ordered prefix of ``documents`` that fits the budget.

    ``documents`` is expected to already be in reranked order. Each document
    contributes at most ``max_excerpt`` characters to the running total, and
    packing stops at the first document that would push the total past
    ``char_budget``.
    """
    packed: list[Document] = []
    used = 0
    for document in documents:
        cost = min(len(document.text), max_excerpt)
        if used + cost > char_budget:
            break
        packed.append(document)
        used += cost
    return packed
evidence.relevance: pack_context implements the bounded, rank-ordered-prefix packing with first-overflow early stop.

questions.prompt: Why must candidates be filtered by tenant in retrieval/rerank.py?
choices: The store intentionally returns candidates across all tenants.
The reranker automatically changes every document's tenant_id.
SearchResponse permits documents from any tenant.
questions.rationale: The candidate store is tenant-blind by design, so the pipeline must enforce tenant isolation.

choices: Immediately after store.candidates() and before rerank.
After rerank and after constructing SearchResponse.
Before store.candidates() is called.
questions.rationale: Filtering immediately after candidate retrieval prevents tenant-blind candidates from reaching later pipeline stages.

questions.prompt: Which implementation preserves the tenant-isolation invariant?
choices: candidates = store.candidates(query); visible = visible_documents_for_tenant(candidates, request.tenant_id); ranked = rerank(visible)
candidates = store.candidates(query); ranked = rerank(candidates); visible = ranked
candidates = store.candidates(query); visible = [doc for doc in candidates if doc.id == request.tenant_id]; ranked = rerank(visible)
questions.rationale: The correct pipeline filters store candidates using the requesting tenant before reranking.

choices: Keep each doc when doc.tenant_id == request.tenant_id.
Keep each doc when doc.tenant_id != request.tenant_id.
Keep every doc regardless of doc.tenant_id.
questions.rationale: Every document that can enter SearchResponse must have the same tenant_id as the request.

choices: Immediately after store.candidates() and before rerank
After rerank but before constructing SearchResponse
After constructing SearchResponse
questions.prompt: Where must retrieval/rerank.py apply visible_documents_for_tenant so tenant-blind store candidates cannot escape or influence reranking?
questions.rationale: Filtering immediately after candidate retrieval establishes tenant isolation before reranking or response construction can expose or process another tenant's documents.

choices: Return only documents where doc.tenant_id == request.tenant_id
Return all candidates and rely on rerank to prioritize the requesting tenant
Return documents where doc.tenant_id is absent or differs from request.tenant_id
questions.prompt: Which implementation preserves the required visibility rule?
questions.rationale: The visibility filter must retain exactly the documents owned by the requesting tenant; reranking is not an authorization boundary.

questions.prompt: Why is MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE kept as one named constant instead of repeating its numeric value wherever a cutoff is needed?
choices: It gives the drop threshold a single authoritative definition, so changing the policy means editing one place and every check stays consistent.
It makes retrieval faster at runtime because named constants are cached more aggressively than literals.
It lets each module in the retrieval pipeline pick its own threshold value independently of the others.
questions.rationale: A single named constant is the single source of truth for the threshold; duplicating the literal lets the checks drift apart and breaks the policy.

questions.prompt: When must apply_confidence_cutoff run relative to context packing?
choices: Before context packing, so documents scoring below the threshold are removed before the context is constructed.
After context packing, so the packer can decide which low-scoring documents are worth keeping.
At either point, because the packer re-checks every document's score against the threshold anyway.
questions.rationale: Running the cutoff before packing ensures weak matches never reach context construction; packing does not re-apply the threshold.

questions.prompt: What does the confidence-cutoff invariant guarantee about retrieval output?
choices: No document with a score below MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE appears in the packed context or the final results.
Every document scoring above MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE must appear, even when the context window is already full.
Documents scoring below MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE may still appear when no higher-scoring documents are available.
questions.rationale: The invariant is a floor: anything below the threshold is excluded from both packed context and final results, with no exceptions.

questions.prompt: A new filter in retrieval/rerank.py was written as `if doc.score > 0.5: keep(doc)`. Which change restores the single-source-of-truth policy without altering the cutoff behavior?
choices: if doc.score >= MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE: keep(doc)
if doc.score > 0.5:  # 0.5 is the same as MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE
if doc.score > 0.3: keep(doc)
questions.rationale: The fix replaces the bare literal with the shared constant and keeps documents at or above the threshold; a comment or a lowered literal still hard-codes the value elsewhere.

questions.prompt: A change rewrites the cutoff as `if doc.score < MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE - 0.05: drop(doc)`. What is wrong with it?
choices: It relaxes the comparison by subtracting a margin, so documents scoring just below MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE survive and violate the invariant.
It is correct, because subtracting a small margin makes the filter more precise near the threshold.
It is wrong only because it calls drop(doc) instead of keep(doc), not because of the threshold math.
questions.rationale: Subtracting a margin lets documents below MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE through, breaking the invariant; the comparison must stay tied to the unmodified constant.

questions.prompt: How should retrieval/rerank.py determine whether a reranked document is too weak to keep?
choices: Compare its score against the named MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE constant
Repeat the current numeric threshold directly at each filtering site
Use a lower local threshold when context still has available space
questions.rationale: MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE must remain the single source of truth so all filtering sites enforce the same policy without duplicated literals or local exceptions.

questions.prompt: Which pipeline behavior preserves the confidence-cutoff invariant?
choices: Pack context first, then remove low-scoring documents from final results
Run apply_confidence_cutoff before context packing and reject scores strictly below MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE
Run apply_confidence_cutoff after context packing and reject only scores far below MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE
questions.rationale: Filtering before context construction prevents weak matches from entering packed context, and the score < MINIMUM_RERANK_SCORE rule preserves documents exactly at the threshold while rejecting every document below it.

questions.prompt: Which invariant must retrieval/context.py preserve when packing reranked documents?
choices: The total is within CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET, every excerpt is within MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS, and packed documents form a prefix of the reranked input.
The total may exceed CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET when individual excerpts stay within MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS.
Documents that do not fit may be skipped so lower-ranked documents can be packed.
questions.rationale: Both character bounds must hold, and packing must preserve a rank-ordered prefix rather than skip documents.

choices: Only in excerpt_for, because pack_context can trust every caller.
Only in pack_context, because excerpt_for may return any length.
In both excerpt_for and pack_context, so each document contribution remains bounded at both layers.
questions.rationale: Both functions must clamp excerpts to MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS to preserve the per-document bound defensively.

questions.prompt: The next reranked document would make the packed total exceed CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET. What should pack_context do?
choices: Stop packing immediately and return the prefix accumulated so far.
Skip that document and try every lower-ranked document.
Add a shortened version even when doing so changes the established excerpt result.
questions.rationale: Packing stops at the first document that would exceed the total budget, preserving a prefix of the reranked input.

questions.prompt: Which code-like behavior correctly clamps one document's excerpt?
choices: excerpt = document.text[:MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS]
excerpt = document.text[:CONTEXT_CHARACTER_BUDGET]
excerpt = document.text if document.rank == 1 else ""
questions.rationale: Slicing at MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS directly enforces the per-document contribution limit.

questions.prompt: pack_context charges each document a cost against the running total before deciding whether it fits char_budget. Which cost expression keeps every excerpt within MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS while still charging a short document only the characters it actually contributes?
choices: min(len(document.text), max_excerpt)
len(document.text)
max_excerpt
questions.rationale: Clamping with min(len(document.text), max_excerpt) caps a long document at the per-excerpt limit while charging a short document only its real length, so both the per-excerpt bound and the budget accounting stay correct. Using len(document.text) lets a single large document exceed MAX_EXCERPT_CHARACTERS and overflow the window; using max_excerpt overcharges short documents and packs fewer than fit.

questions.prompt: A teammate's pack_context skips any document whose cost would exceed char_budget and keeps scanning later, smaller documents. The packed list is no longer a rank-ordered prefix. Which complete loop body restores the invariant?
choices: cost = min(len(document.text), max_excerpt); if used + cost > char_budget: break; packed.append(document); used += cost
cost = min(len(document.text), max_excerpt); if used + cost > char_budget: continue; packed.append(document); used += cost
cost = min(len(document.text), max_excerpt); packed.append(document); used += cost; if used > char_budget: break
questions.rationale: Breaking at the first document that would exceed the budget keeps the result a prefix of the reranked input. The continue version skips the overflowing document and admits a lower-ranked one behind it, breaking the prefix property; the third version appends before checking, so the total can exceed char_budget by one document before stopping.
